name: Vision-Language-Action Loop
summary: An agent perceives through vision, reasons through language, and acts through UI automation in a continuous loop.
description: The agent operates in a tight perception-reasoning-action cycle: it observes the environment through screenshots or camera feeds, reasons about the current state using a vision-language model, selects and executes an action (click, keystroke, API call), then observes the result. The loop runs continuously until the task is complete or the agent gets stuck and escalates. The key challenge is grounding -- the model must accurately map visual observations to actionable understanding of UI state, including detecting when an action succeeded or failed.
realWorldExample: Anthropic's computer use feature lets Claude see screenshots and control mouse/keyboard to complete tasks on a desktop. Google's Project Mariner navigates web pages visually. Apple Intelligence uses on-screen context to understand and act on what the user sees. UI-TARS (ByteDance) and CogAgent (Tsinghua) are open-source vision-language-action models for GUI agents. These systems share the same core loop: screenshot → reason → act → screenshot.
whenToUse: When the agent must interact with systems that have no API -- legacy desktop apps, web UIs without stable selectors, or physical environments with camera input. Also useful when visual understanding provides richer context than DOM parsing or API responses alone.
strengths: Works with any visual interface -- no API or structured access required
Visual grounding provides richer context than text-only approaches (layout, color, state indicators)
The continuous loop is self-correcting -- the agent sees whether its action succeeded
Generalizes across applications without per-app integration work
weaknesses: Screenshot-based perception is slower and more expensive than API calls
Visual grounding errors (misidentifying UI elements) can cascade into wrong actions
Latency of the observe-reason-act cycle limits task speed
Brittle to UI changes -- screen resolution, theme, or layout shifts break learned visual patterns

agents.name: Visual Perceiver
agents.description: Captures screenshots or camera frames and preprocesses them for the vision-language model. Handles screen region selection, resolution normalization, and frame timing

agents.name: Language Reasoner
agents.description: A vision-language model that interprets the current visual state, determines task progress, and selects the next action from an action space (click coordinates, keystrokes, scroll, type text)

agents.name: Action Executor
agents.description: Translates the reasoner's high-level action selection into concrete OS-level events (mouse moves, key presses, API calls) and confirms execution

shared: Task specification -- the user's goal and any constraints
Action history -- sequence of (screenshot, action, result) triples for context
private: Reasoner's internal chain-of-thought about UI state and next steps
memoryArchitecture.description: The perceiver and executor are stateless. The reasoner maintains a sliding window of recent observation-action pairs as working memory. Long-term task context comes from the original task specification and accumulated action history.

diagram:
graph TD
  T[Task Goal] --> R[Language Reasoner]
  P[Visual Perceiver] -->|screenshot| R
  R -->|action selection| E[Action Executor]
  E -->|mouse/keyboard/API| ENV[Environment]
  ENV -->|visual feedback| P
  R -->|done?| CHECK{Task Complete?}
  CHECK -->|no| P
  CHECK -->|yes| OUT[Result]

name: Multimodal Routing
summary: An input classifier detects modality (text, image, audio, video, code) and routes to specialist processors.
description: A dispatcher agent examines incoming requests to determine the modality mix -- text, image, audio, video, code, or combinations. It routes each modality component to a specialist processor optimized for that type. For multimodal inputs (e.g., an image with a text question), the dispatcher may invoke multiple specialists in parallel and synthesize their outputs. The pattern decouples modality-specific processing from the orchestration logic, allowing each specialist to be upgraded or swapped independently.
realWorldExample: GPT-4o processes text, images, and audio natively in one model, performing internal multimodal routing. Google Gemini handles text, images, video, and audio with unified multimodal understanding. For multi-agent systems, Semantic Kernel's magentic orchestration can route based on content type. Many production systems use a text classifier to decide whether to invoke vision, audio transcription, or code execution agents.
whenToUse: When your system must handle diverse input types and the optimal processing strategy differs by modality. Especially valuable when specialists have different cost, latency, or accuracy profiles and you want to avoid sending everything through a single expensive multimodal model.
strengths: Each specialist can be independently optimized, scaled, and upgraded for its modality
Cost-efficient -- simple text queries skip expensive vision or audio processing
Parallel specialist invocation reduces latency for mixed-modality inputs
New modalities can be added by registering a new specialist without changing the router
weaknesses: The input classifier is a single point of failure -- misclassification sends input to the wrong specialist
Cross-modal reasoning (questions that require understanding image AND text together) can be lost when splitting
Synthesis of specialist outputs adds complexity and can lose nuance
Maintaining multiple specialist models is operationally expensive

agents.name: Modality Classifier
agents.description: Examines incoming requests to determine modality composition (text, image, audio, video, code) and routes to appropriate specialists

agents.name: Text Specialist
agents.description: Processes text-only inputs -- summarization, QA, generation, classification

agents.name: Vision Specialist
agents.description: Processes image and video inputs -- captioning, object detection, visual QA, OCR

agents.name: Audio Specialist
agents.description: Processes audio inputs -- transcription, speaker identification, tone analysis

agents.name: Response Synthesizer
agents.description: Combines outputs from multiple specialists into a unified response for multi-modal inputs

shared: Request context -- the original user request with all modality components
Specialist outputs -- intermediate results from each modality processor
memoryArchitecture.description: The classifier is stateless. Specialists process independently and return structured outputs. The synthesizer receives all specialist outputs and the original request context to produce a coherent response. No persistent state is needed across requests.

diagram:
graph TD
  IN[Input Request] --> MC[Modality Classifier]
  MC -->|text| TS[Text Specialist]
  MC -->|image/video| VS[Vision Specialist]
  MC -->|audio| AS[Audio Specialist]
  MC -->|code| CS[Code Specialist]
  TS --> SYN[Response Synthesizer]
  VS --> SYN
  AS --> SYN
  CS --> SYN
  SYN --> OUT[Unified Response]

name: Knowledge Graph-Grounded Agent
summary: The agent reasons over a structured knowledge graph for typed relationships and consistency that flat text retrieval cannot provide.
description: The agent's reasoning is grounded in a structured knowledge graph rather than (or in addition to) vector-retrieved text. When the agent needs information, it translates natural language queries into graph queries (Cypher, SPARQL, or similar), traverses typed relationships, and uses the structured results as context for generation. The graph provides transitive reasoning (A relates to B, B relates to C, therefore A connects to C), consistency checking (contradictions are structurally visible), and relationship typing that flat text chunks cannot express.
realWorldExample: Klarna uses a Neo4j knowledge graph ('Kiki') alongside RAG for their AI assistant, providing structured product and policy relationships. Microsoft's GraphRAG builds a knowledge graph from documents before querying, enabling multi-hop reasoning across document boundaries. Amazon Neptune provides graph-based knowledge for Bedrock agents. Neo4j's LLM integration enables Cypher query generation from natural language.
whenToUse: When your domain has rich interconnected relationships that matter for reasoning -- organizational hierarchies, product catalogs, regulatory frameworks, medical ontologies. Especially valuable when answers require multi-hop reasoning across entities or when consistency and auditability matter more than speed.
strengths: Typed relationships enable precise multi-hop reasoning (follow chains of specific relationship types)
Consistency is structurally enforced -- contradictions are visible as conflicting edges
Graph queries return exactly relevant subgraphs, avoiding the noise of vector similarity search
The knowledge graph serves as an auditable, explainable source of truth
weaknesses: Building and maintaining a knowledge graph requires significant upfront investment
Natural language to graph query translation (text-to-Cypher) is itself an error-prone LLM task
Graphs struggle with unstructured or ambiguous knowledge that doesn't fit clean entity-relationship models
Schema evolution is painful -- adding new relationship types can require reindexing

agents.name: Query Translator
agents.description: Converts natural language questions into graph queries (Cypher, SPARQL) using the graph schema as context

agents.name: Graph Executor
agents.description: Executes graph queries against the knowledge graph database and returns structured subgraphs or result sets

agents.name: Reasoning Agent
agents.description: Uses retrieved graph context alongside the original question to generate grounded responses with citations to specific entities and relationships

shared: Knowledge graph -- the persistent structured store of entities and relationships
Graph schema -- node types, relationship types, and property definitions
private: Query translator's schema understanding and query patterns
memoryArchitecture.description: The knowledge graph is the persistent shared memory. The query translator uses the schema to generate valid queries. The reasoning agent receives structured graph results as context. No agent modifies the graph during query -- updates happen through a separate ingestion pipeline.

diagram:
graph TD
  Q[User Question] --> QT[Query Translator]
  SCHEMA[(Graph Schema)] -.->|guides| QT
  QT -->|Cypher/SPARQL| GE[Graph Executor]
  KG[(Knowledge Graph)] <-->|query/results| GE
  GE -->|structured subgraph| RA[Reasoning Agent]
  Q -->|original question| RA
  RA --> OUT[Grounded Response]

name: Retrieval-Augmented Fine-Tuning
summary: Domain knowledge is fine-tuned into the model itself rather than retrieved at inference time.
description: Instead of retrieving relevant context at inference time (RAG), domain-specific knowledge is curated into training data and used to fine-tune the model. The model internalizes domain terminology, reasoning patterns, and factual knowledge. This trades inference-time flexibility for faster, cheaper, more consistent responses within a known domain. The pipeline has distinct phases: data curation → fine-tuning → evaluation → deployment, with the fine-tuned model replacing or supplementing the base model for domain-specific tasks.
realWorldExample: OpenAI's fine-tuning API accepts custom training data in JSONL format for domain adaptation. Anthropic offers fine-tuned Claude models for specific enterprise use cases. Together AI and Anyscale provide fine-tuning services for open-weight models. The pattern is used when the domain is stable and retrieval latency or cost at inference time is prohibitive -- medical coding, legal contract analysis, and financial report generation are common use cases.
whenToUse: When your domain is well-defined and stable (not rapidly changing), when inference latency or cost from retrieval is prohibitive, when you need consistent tone and terminology, or when the knowledge base is small enough to internalize but too specialized for the base model.
strengths: Faster inference -- no retrieval step, embedding lookup, or context stuffing at query time
More consistent outputs -- internalized knowledge produces more uniform style and terminology
Lower per-query cost -- no vector database queries or large context windows needed
The model 'understands' the domain rather than pattern-matching against retrieved chunks
weaknesses: Knowledge is frozen at training time -- the model cannot reflect new information without retraining
Fine-tuning is expensive and slow compared to updating a retrieval index
Overfitting risk -- the model may lose general capabilities when over-trained on narrow data
No citation or attribution -- the model cannot point to where it learned something

agents.name: Data Curator
agents.description: Selects, cleans, and formats domain-specific training examples into prompt-completion pairs suitable for fine-tuning

agents.name: Fine-Tuning Pipeline
agents.description: Manages the training process -- hyperparameter selection, training runs, checkpoint evaluation, and model selection

agents.name: Evaluation Agent
agents.description: Tests the fine-tuned model against held-out domain examples and regression benchmarks to ensure quality before deployment

agents.name: Deployment Agent
agents.description: Manages model versioning, A/B testing between base and fine-tuned models, and gradual traffic shifting

shared: Training dataset -- curated domain examples in prompt-completion format
Evaluation benchmarks -- held-out test sets and regression suites
Model registry -- versioned fine-tuned model checkpoints with metadata
private: Curator's domain knowledge and data selection criteria
memoryArchitecture.description: The training data and evaluation benchmarks are shared artifacts. The fine-tuning pipeline produces versioned model checkpoints. The evaluation agent compares each checkpoint against benchmarks. The deployment agent selects the best model for production. Knowledge flows in one direction: data → model → deployment.

diagram:
graph LR
  RAW[Domain Knowledge] --> DC[Data Curator]
  DC -->|training pairs| FT[Fine-Tuning Pipeline]
  FT -->|model checkpoint| EV[Evaluation Agent]
  BENCH[(Benchmarks)] -.-> EV
  EV -->|pass/fail| DA[Deployment Agent]
  DA -->|deploy| PROD[Production Model]
  EV -->|fail - iterate| DC

name: Agent Mesh Network
summary: Agents communicate peer-to-peer without a central coordinator, self-organizing into a resilient mesh.
description: Multiple agents communicate directly with each other through peer-to-peer connections without any central orchestrator. Each agent maintains connections to a subset of neighbors and can relay messages to reach distant nodes. The mesh is self-organizing -- agents can join, leave, or fail without disrupting the network, because routing adapts dynamically. Consensus on shared state emerges from local interactions and gossip protocols rather than top-down coordination. The pattern trades coordination efficiency for resilience and decentralization.
realWorldExample: Fetch.ai's autonomous economic agents communicate via a decentralized peer-to-peer network for multi-agent coordination. The NEAR AI agent protocol enables agent-to-agent communication on blockchain infrastructure. CrewAI's hierarchical=False mode enables peer communication between agents. Research systems like ChatArena and AgentVerse implement mesh topologies for agent debate and collaboration.
whenToUse: When no single point of failure is acceptable, when agents must operate independently and coordinate opportunistically, or when the agent population is dynamic (agents frequently join and leave). Also appropriate when the problem is naturally decomposable into local interactions without global state.
strengths: No single point of failure -- the network survives individual agent failures
Dynamic membership -- agents can join or leave without reconfiguring the system
Scales horizontally -- adding agents increases capacity without bottlenecking a coordinator
Resilient to partitions -- subgroups can continue operating independently and reconcile later
weaknesses: Consensus is slow and expensive -- distributed agreement requires multiple message rounds
No global view -- no single agent knows the full system state at any moment
Message routing through multiple hops adds latency compared to direct orchestration
Debugging is difficult -- emergent behavior from local interactions is hard to trace

agents.name: Mesh Agent
agents.description: A peer in the network that maintains connections to neighbors, processes tasks, relays messages, and participates in gossip-based state synchronization

agents.name: Bootstrap Node
agents.description: An initial contact point that helps new agents discover existing peers and join the mesh. Not a coordinator -- once connected, the new agent operates independently

shared: Gossip state -- eventually-consistent shared knowledge propagated through neighbor exchanges
Peer directory -- known agents and their connection endpoints
private: Each agent's local task state and partial view of the network
Connection state -- which neighbors are reachable and their last-known status
memoryArchitecture.description: There is no centralized shared memory. Each agent maintains a local view of the network state that converges eventually through gossip. The bootstrap node provides initial discovery but does not maintain authoritative state. Consistency is eventual, not immediate.

diagram:
graph TD
  A1[Agent 1] <--> A2[Agent 2]
  A1 <--> A3[Agent 3]
  A2 <--> A4[Agent 4]
  A2 <--> A5[Agent 5]
  A3 <--> A5
  A4 <--> A6[Agent 6]
  A5 <--> A6
  A3 <--> A7[Agent 7]
  A7 <--> A6
  BN[Bootstrap Node] -.->|discovery| A1
  BN -.->|discovery| A4

name: Event-Driven Agent Bus
summary: Agents subscribe to typed events on a shared bus, activating independently when relevant events occur.
description: Agents subscribe to events on a shared message bus rather than being called directly. When an event is published, all subscribed agents are activated independently. Multiple agents can react to the same event, enabling parallel processing without explicit coordination. Agents communicate by emitting new events rather than calling each other, fully decoupling producers from consumers. The bus handles delivery, ordering, and replay. This pattern turns agent coordination into an event-flow architecture.
realWorldExample: LlamaIndex Workflows uses an event-driven architecture where agents emit and listen for typed events through a shared context. Temporal's workflow engine treats agent invocations as event-driven activities with durable execution guarantees. Inngest provides event-driven serverless functions that can wrap agent logic with automatic retries and fan-out. Apache Kafka is used as an agent event bus in production systems that need message durability and replay.
whenToUse: When agents need to react to changes rather than be explicitly orchestrated, when multiple agents should independently process the same event, or when you want to add new agents without modifying existing ones. Best for systems where the workflow is defined by what happens rather than who coordinates.
strengths: Fully decoupled -- agents don't know about each other, only about event types
Adding new agents is zero-cost to existing agents -- just subscribe to relevant events
Natural parallelism -- multiple agents process the same event concurrently
Event replay enables debugging, auditing, and recovery from failures
weaknesses: Complex event chains are hard to reason about -- cause-and-effect spans multiple agents
No global workflow visibility -- the execution path is emergent from subscriptions
Event ordering and exactly-once delivery are hard distributed systems problems
Debugging requires tracing events across multiple agents and the bus

agents.name: Event Producer
agents.description: Publishes typed events to the bus when significant state changes or actions occur

agents.name: Event Consumer
agents.description: Subscribes to specific event types and activates when matching events arrive. May produce new events as output

agents.name: Bus Manager
agents.description: Manages the event bus infrastructure -- delivery guarantees, ordering, dead-letter queues, and replay capabilities

shared: Event log -- the durable, ordered record of all events published to the bus
Subscription registry -- which agents subscribe to which event types
private: Each consumer's processing state and offset (how far it has read in the event log)
memoryArchitecture.description: The event bus is the central shared memory, but no agent owns or controls it. Producers write events, consumers read them at their own pace. The bus provides durability and replay. Each consumer maintains its own offset and processing state privately.

diagram:
graph TD
  EP1[Producer A] -->|event: task.created| BUS[[Event Bus]]
  EP2[Producer B] -->|event: data.updated| BUS
  BUS -->|task.created| EC1[Consumer 1]
  BUS -->|task.created| EC2[Consumer 2]
  BUS -->|data.updated| EC3[Consumer 3]
  EC1 -->|event: task.processed| BUS
  EC3 -->|event: alert.triggered| BUS
  BUS -->|alert.triggered| EC4[Consumer 4]
  DLQ[(Dead Letter Queue)] -.-> BUS

name: Rate-Limit-Aware Orchestrator
summary: The orchestrator tracks API rate limits across providers and routes requests to balance load, avoid throttling, and minimize cost.
description: The orchestrator maintains awareness of rate limits, quotas, and pricing across multiple LLM providers. When agents request LLM calls, the orchestrator routes them to the optimal provider based on current utilization, remaining quota, cost per token, and latency. It can queue requests during rate limit windows, retry with exponential backoff, fall back to alternative models, or batch requests for throughput. Rate limit management is a first-class architectural concern rather than error handling in individual agents.
realWorldExample: LiteLLM provides a unified API that load-balances across 100+ LLM providers with automatic retries, fallback, and cost tracking. OpenRouter routes requests across providers with cost optimization and model selection. Helicone provides rate-limit-aware proxying with caching and analytics. Production multi-agent systems that make hundreds of LLM calls per task must handle rate limiting architecturally -- Stripe's agent system routes across providers to maintain throughput during peak load.
whenToUse: When your agent system makes enough LLM calls that rate limits are a real constraint, when you use multiple LLM providers and want to optimize cost or availability, or when task completion time is sensitive to API throttling. Essential for any production multi-agent system.
strengths: Prevents cascading failures from rate limit exhaustion during complex multi-agent tasks
Cost optimization through intelligent provider routing based on real-time pricing
Automatic fallback ensures tasks complete even when primary providers are throttled
Centralized tracking provides visibility into LLM spend and utilization across all agents
weaknesses: The orchestrator itself is a single point of failure for all LLM access
Model quality varies across providers -- fallback models may produce lower-quality outputs
Rate limit tracking requires real-time state management and adds latency to every call
Provider API differences (context length, tool support, output format) complicate routing

agents.name: Rate Limit Tracker
agents.description: Monitors rate limit headers and usage across all configured providers. Maintains real-time counters for requests, tokens, and quota windows

agents.name: Request Router
agents.description: Receives LLM call requests from agents and routes them to the optimal provider based on current limits, cost, latency, and model capability requirements

agents.name: Retry Manager
agents.description: Handles failed requests with exponential backoff, provider fallback, and request queuing during rate limit windows

agents.name: Cost Tracker
agents.description: Tracks per-request costs, maintains budget alerts, and provides spend analytics across providers and agents

shared: Provider registry -- endpoints, rate limits, pricing, model capabilities for each provider
Usage counters -- real-time request and token counts per provider per time window
Cost ledger -- cumulative spend by provider, model, and requesting agent
private: Retry queue -- pending requests waiting for rate limit windows to reset
memoryArchitecture.description: The rate limit tracker and cost tracker maintain shared state that all routing decisions depend on. Usage counters are updated on every request and reset on window boundaries. The request router is stateless -- it reads current state and makes routing decisions. The retry manager maintains a private queue of deferred requests.

diagram:
graph TD
  A1[Agent 1] -->|LLM request| RR[Request Router]
  A2[Agent 2] -->|LLM request| RR
  A3[Agent 3] -->|LLM request| RR
  RLT[(Rate Limit Tracker)] -.->|current limits| RR
  CT[(Cost Tracker)] -.->|budget status| RR
  RR -->|route| P1[Provider A\nOpenAI]
  RR -->|route| P2[Provider B\nAnthropic]
  RR -->|route| P3[Provider C\nOpen-weight]
  RR -->|throttled| RM[Retry Manager]
  RM -->|retry after backoff| RR
  P1 -->|response + headers| RLT
  P2 -->|response + headers| RLT
  P3 -->|response + headers| RLT

name: Agent Constitution
summary: A versioned formal document defines the agent's values, boundaries, permitted actions, and escalation policies.
description: A formal constitution document defines the agent system's values, boundaries, permitted actions, forbidden actions, and escalation policies. The constitution is loaded into system prompts and enforced by dedicated guardrail agents. Unlike ad-hoc safety prompting scattered across agents, the constitution is versioned, auditable, and shared as a single source of truth. Changes to the constitution go through a review process. Guardrail agents check outputs against the constitution before they reach users.
realWorldExample: Anthropic's Constitutional AI defines principles that guide self-critique and revision, training the model to evaluate its own outputs against explicit values. Salesforce Agentforce uses 'Topics' containing business policies and guardrails as constitutional documents that constrain agent behavior per-domain. Claude's system prompt and usage policy function as a constitution. Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics are the original fictional agent constitution.
whenToUse: When agents operate in regulated domains (healthcare, finance, legal), when safety and compliance must be auditable, when multiple agents need consistent behavior boundaries, or when organizational policies must be enforced uniformly across an agent fleet.
strengths: Single source of truth for agent behavior -- all agents follow the same explicit rules
Versioned and auditable -- policy changes are tracked, reviewable, and rollback-able
Separation of concerns -- the constitution is maintained by policy experts, not developers
Guardrail agents provide runtime enforcement, not just prompt-time aspiration
weaknesses: Constitutions cannot anticipate every situation -- edge cases require human judgment
Overly restrictive constitutions reduce agent usefulness (false positive refusals)
Constitutional AI self-critique adds latency to every agent response
The constitution itself is a prompt -- LLMs can still violate it through adversarial inputs

agents.name: Constitution Author
agents.description: Drafts and maintains the constitution document. Reviews proposed changes, manages versioning, and ensures internal consistency

agents.name: Guardrail Agent
agents.description: Evaluates agent outputs against the constitution before they reach users. Flags violations, suggests revisions, or blocks responses

agents.name: Operational Agent
agents.description: Any agent in the system that has the constitution loaded into its system prompt and is subject to guardrail review

agents.name: Escalation Handler
agents.description: Receives cases that the guardrail agent cannot resolve -- ambiguous situations, novel edge cases, or high-stakes decisions that require human review

shared: Constitution -- the versioned policy document loaded into all agent system prompts
Violation log -- record of all flagged outputs with the violated principle and resolution
private: Guardrail agent's evaluation criteria and threshold calibration
Escalation handler's case history and resolution precedents
memoryArchitecture.description: The constitution is the shared bedrock. All operational agents receive it in their system prompt. The guardrail agent applies it as evaluation criteria. The violation log feeds back to the constitution author for policy refinement. Escalation precedents accumulate in the handler's append-only memory.

diagram:
graph TD
  CA[Constitution Author] -->|version & publish| CONST[(Constitution\nv2.3)]
  CONST -.->|system prompt| OA1[Operational Agent 1]
  CONST -.->|system prompt| OA2[Operational Agent 2]
  CONST -.->|evaluation criteria| GA[Guardrail Agent]
  OA1 -->|output| GA
  OA2 -->|output| GA
  GA -->|approved| USER[User]
  GA -->|violation| VL[(Violation Log)]
  GA -->|ambiguous| EH[Escalation Handler]
  EH -->|precedent| CA

name: Agent Marketplace
summary: Agent capabilities are published to a registry where other agents discover, evaluate, and invoke them on demand.
description: Agents or agent capabilities are published to a shared registry with capability descriptions, API contracts, pricing, and quality metrics. Consuming agents discover relevant capabilities by searching the registry, evaluate options based on cost, quality, and latency, and invoke them through standardized interfaces. Complex workflows are assembled by composing capabilities from multiple providers. The marketplace handles discovery, contract negotiation, billing, and quality assurance. The pattern mirrors app stores but for agent capabilities.
realWorldExample: OpenAI's GPT Store lets users publish and discover custom GPTs with ratings and usage metrics. Anthropic's MCP server ecosystem is an emerging tool marketplace where servers advertise capabilities and clients discover them. Hugging Face Spaces hosts thousands of specialized AI endpoints with standardized inference APIs. Zapier's AI Actions expose 30,000+ app integrations as agent-callable tools. The pattern enables composition without building everything from scratch.
whenToUse: When no single team can build all needed capabilities, when you want to leverage a ecosystem of specialized providers, or when agents need to dynamically discover and compose capabilities they weren't explicitly designed to use. Best for open-ended systems where the task space is broader than any one agent's abilities.
strengths: Composability -- complex workflows assembled from existing capabilities without custom development
Specialization incentive -- providers compete on quality and cost within their niche
Dynamic discovery lets agents handle novel tasks by finding relevant capabilities at runtime
Standardized contracts reduce integration cost between independently developed agents
weaknesses: Quality variance across providers -- marketplace agents may have inconsistent reliability
Security risk -- invoking third-party capabilities exposes data to external agents
Discovery overhead -- searching, evaluating, and selecting capabilities adds latency to every task
Vendor lock-in to the marketplace platform itself (API format, billing, discovery protocol)

agents.name: Capability Provider
agents.description: Publishes agent capabilities to the registry with descriptions, API contracts, pricing, and SLA guarantees

agents.name: Capability Consumer
agents.description: Searches the registry for capabilities matching its current task needs, evaluates options, and invokes selected providers

agents.name: Registry Manager
agents.description: Maintains the capability catalog, handles registration, search indexing, quality scoring, and de-listing of underperforming providers

agents.name: Contract Enforcer
agents.description: Monitors invocations for SLA compliance, handles billing, mediates disputes between providers and consumers

shared: Capability catalog -- searchable registry of all published capabilities with metadata
Quality metrics -- usage counts, success rates, latency percentiles, user ratings per capability
Contract templates -- standardized SLA and pricing structures
private: Provider's internal implementation and proprietary models
Consumer's task context and selection criteria
memoryArchitecture.description: The registry is the shared memory that connects providers and consumers. Quality metrics accumulate from invocation outcomes. Providers maintain private implementations behind standardized interfaces. Consumers maintain private task context that informs their capability selection.

diagram:
graph TD
  CP1[Provider A\nCode Review] -->|register| REG[(Capability Registry)]
  CP2[Provider B\nTranslation] -->|register| REG
  CP3[Provider C\nData Analysis] -->|register| REG
  CC1[Consumer Agent] -->|search| REG
  REG -->|matches| CC1
  CC1 -->|invoke| CP2
  CC1 -->|invoke| CP3
  CP2 -->|result| CC1
  CP3 -->|result| CC1
  CE[Contract Enforcer] -.->|monitor SLA| CP2
  CE -.->|monitor SLA| CP3
  CC1 -->|quality rating| REG

name: Simulated Environment Training
summary: Agents learn and are evaluated in simulated environments that mirror production before graduating to live deployment.
description: Agents are trained and evaluated in simulated environments that mirror production conditions with deterministic, reproducible feedback. The simulation provides controlled scenarios covering normal operations, edge cases, and failure modes. Agents that pass simulation benchmarks progress to shadow mode (real inputs, discarded outputs) before full production. The simulation environment is the first line of quality assurance, catching behavioral issues before they affect real users. The pipeline is: train in simulation → benchmark evaluation → shadow mode → production deployment.
realWorldExample: Meta's Voyager agents learn in Minecraft simulations, developing skills through interaction with a game environment. WebArena and VisualWebArena provide standardized web environments for evaluating browser agents on realistic tasks. OSWorld provides a desktop simulation for testing computer-use agents across operating systems. SWE-bench tests coding agents on real GitHub issues in sandboxed repositories with automated pass/fail evaluation.
whenToUse: When agent failures in production are costly or dangerous, when you need reproducible evaluation of agent behavior, when training data must come from interaction rather than static datasets, or when you want to compare agent architectures on standardized benchmarks.
strengths: Reproducible evaluation -- the same simulation produces the same test conditions every time
Safe failure -- agents can fail in simulation without real-world consequences
Automated benchmarking enables objective comparison of agent architectures and training approaches
Shadow mode provides real-world validation without risk before full deployment
weaknesses: Simulation fidelity gap -- the simulation never perfectly matches production conditions
Building and maintaining realistic simulations is expensive and time-consuming
Agents may overfit to simulation quirks rather than learning generalizable behaviors
The sim-to-real transfer problem -- behaviors learned in simulation may not transfer to production

agents.name: Environment Simulator
agents.description: Maintains the simulated environment -- renders state, processes agent actions, generates deterministic feedback, and tracks scenario progress

agents.name: Trainee Agent
agents.description: The agent being trained or evaluated. Interacts with the simulated environment through the same interface it will use in production

agents.name: Benchmark Evaluator
agents.description: Runs the trainee through standardized test scenarios, scores performance on predefined metrics, and determines pass/fail for promotion

agents.name: Shadow Monitor
agents.description: In shadow mode, feeds real production inputs to the trainee and compares its proposed actions against production outcomes without executing them

shared: Scenario library -- deterministic test scenarios with expected outcomes
Evaluation metrics -- scoring rubrics, pass thresholds, and regression baselines
Promotion criteria -- requirements for advancing from simulation to shadow to production
private: Trainee agent's learned behaviors and internal state
Shadow monitor's comparison logs between trainee proposals and production outcomes
memoryArchitecture.description: The scenario library and evaluation metrics are shared standards. The trainee interacts with the simulation through a production-identical interface. The evaluator scores against shared rubrics. Shadow mode comparison logs accumulate privately until the trainee demonstrates consistent production-quality behavior.

diagram:
graph LR
  SIM[Simulated Environment] <-->|observe/act| TA[Trainee Agent]
  SCEN[(Scenario Library)] -.-> SIM
  TA --> EV[Benchmark Evaluator]
  EV -->|pass| SM[Shadow Monitor]
  EV -->|fail - retrain| TA
  PROD[Production Traffic] -.->|mirror input| SM
  SM -->|compare| REPORT[(Evaluation Reports)]
  SM -->|pass shadow| DEPLOY[Production Deploy]
  SM -->|fail shadow| TA

name: Streaming Thought Display
summary: The agent streams its reasoning process to the user in real-time -- tool calls, intermediate results, decisions -- rather than waiting for a final polished answer.
description: The agent's internal reasoning process is exposed to the user as it happens. Tool calls are shown as they execute, intermediate results appear as they arrive, and the agent's decision-making is narrated in real-time. The user sees progress, can interrupt or redirect mid-reasoning, and gains trust through transparency. The pattern requires a streaming transport layer that can push partial results, a UI that renders incremental updates, and careful chunking of the reasoning pipeline into displayable steps. The trade-off: transparency may reveal messiness that a polished final answer would hide.
realWorldExample: Claude's extended thinking shows reasoning before the answer. ChatGPT's 'browsing...' and 'analyzing...' status indicators. Perplexity shows sources as they're found. Claude Code streams tool calls and their results live. This is the dominant UX pattern for agent transparency.
whenToUse: When user trust requires visibility into the agent's process, when tasks are long-running and users need progress feedback, when the ability to interrupt or redirect mid-reasoning is valuable, or when debugging agent behavior requires seeing the full reasoning chain.
strengths: Builds user trust by showing the agent is working and how it is reasoning
Enables mid-course correction -- users can interrupt and redirect before wasted work
Perceived latency drops dramatically even when total time is unchanged
Streaming output serves as a debugging tool for agent developers
weaknesses: Exposes reasoning messiness -- dead ends and retries are visible to users
Streaming infrastructure adds complexity (WebSocket/SSE transport, incremental rendering)
Users may over-react to intermediate steps that look wrong but would self-correct
Token-by-token streaming can be distracting for tasks where the final answer is all that matters

agents.name: Reasoning Engine
agents.description: The core agent that processes the user's request, making tool calls and producing reasoning steps that are emitted as a stream

agents.name: Stream Formatter
agents.description: Transforms raw reasoning events (tool calls, partial results, decisions) into user-facing display chunks with appropriate formatting and status labels

agents.name: Interrupt Handler
agents.description: Monitors for user interrupts during streaming and gracefully halts the reasoning pipeline, preserving partial results for the user to review or redirect

shared: Streaming buffer -- the ordered sequence of reasoning events awaiting display
Task context -- the user's request and any accumulated partial results
private: Reasoning engine's internal chain-of-thought and tool call history
memoryArchitecture.description: The reasoning engine emits events into a streaming buffer. The formatter reads and transforms them for display. The interrupt handler can inject stop signals into the buffer. No persistent memory is needed -- each request is a fresh stream. The key architectural decision is granularity: how much reasoning detail to expose per stream chunk.

diagram:
graph LR
  USER[User Request] --> RE[Reasoning Engine]
  RE -->|tool call| TOOL[Tool Execution]
  TOOL -->|result| RE
  RE -->|reasoning event| SF[Stream Formatter]
  SF -->|display chunk| UI[User Interface]
  UI -->|interrupt| IH[Interrupt Handler]
  IH -->|stop signal| RE
  RE -->|final answer| SF

name: Real-Time Collaboration Agent
summary: Multiple human users and AI agents collaborate on the same artifact simultaneously with conflict resolution via CRDTs.
description: Multiple human users and AI agents collaborate on the same artifact simultaneously. The agent sees real-time edits from all participants and can contribute alongside them. Conflict resolution happens through CRDTs (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types) or operational transforms -- the same mechanisms used for human-human collaboration. The agent is a peer in the editing session, not a separate tool invoked from outside. The pattern requires awareness of cursor positions, selection state, and concurrent edit intent to avoid stepping on other participants' work.
realWorldExample: Cursor's multi-file editing with live preview. Google Docs AI integration where Gemini edits alongside human collaborators. Figma AI generates designs in the shared canvas. Replit's multiplayer + Agent mode where the agent and user edit the same codebase concurrently. Liveblocks provides the CRDT infrastructure many of these use.
whenToUse: When the work product is a shared artifact that benefits from simultaneous human and AI contribution, when iteration speed matters more than review gates, when multiple agents or humans need to see each other's changes immediately, or when the collaboration pattern mirrors pair programming or design sessions.
strengths: Fastest iteration loop -- agent changes are visible instantly without submit/review cycles
Conflict resolution is automatic through CRDTs, no manual merge needed
The agent benefits from real-time human context (cursor position, selection, active area)
Natural extension of existing multiplayer collaboration tools
weaknesses: The agent may overwrite or conflict with human edits in progress
CRDT infrastructure adds significant technical complexity
Real-time awareness of other participants requires constant state synchronization
Hard to undo agent changes that interleave with human edits

agents.name: Collaboration Agent
agents.description: An AI participant in the shared editing session. Observes all participants' activity, contributes edits through the same CRDT layer, and respects cursor proximity to avoid conflicts with active human work

agents.name: Presence Tracker
agents.description: Maintains awareness of all participants' cursor positions, selections, and active editing regions. Provides spatial context to the collaboration agent to avoid stepping on human work

agents.name: Conflict Resolver
agents.description: Manages the CRDT or operational transform layer that merges concurrent edits from all participants into a consistent shared state

shared: Document state -- the CRDT-backed shared artifact that all participants edit
Presence map -- real-time cursor positions, selections, and activity indicators for all participants
private: Collaboration agent's intent model -- what it plans to edit and why, used to coordinate with presence data
memoryArchitecture.description: The shared document state is the single source of truth, maintained by the CRDT layer. Presence data is broadcast to all participants in real-time. The collaboration agent reads presence data to avoid conflicting with human work. All edits flow through the same conflict resolution mechanism regardless of whether the author is human or AI.

diagram:
graph TD
  H1[Human User 1] -->|edits| CRDT[CRDT Layer]
  H2[Human User 2] -->|edits| CRDT
  CA[Collaboration Agent] -->|edits| CRDT
  CRDT <-->|sync| DOC[(Shared Document)]
  PT[Presence Tracker] -->|cursor positions| CA
  H1 -->|cursor/selection| PT
  H2 -->|cursor/selection| PT
  CRDT -->|merged state| H1
  CRDT -->|merged state| H2
  CRDT -->|merged state| CA

name: Memory Consolidation Cycle
summary: An agent periodically reviews accumulated memories, merging redundant ones, updating stale ones, and promoting frequently-accessed patterns.
description: An agent periodically reviews its accumulated memories, merges redundant ones, updates stale ones, removes contradicted ones, and promotes frequently-accessed patterns to faster retrieval. This is garbage collection for agent memory -- without it, memory grows unboundedly and retrieval quality degrades as noise accumulates. The consolidation cycle runs on a schedule or trigger (memory count threshold, session boundary), not on every interaction. The pattern requires the ability to compare memories for semantic similarity, detect contradictions, and decide which memories are worth keeping.
realWorldExample: The forge.thisminute.org memory system instructs agents to 'update or remove memories that turn out to be wrong or outdated' and 'do not write duplicate memories.' MemGPT (now Letta) implements tiered memory with automatic consolidation between working memory and archival storage. Generative agents (Stanford) use reflection to consolidate observations into higher-level insights.
whenToUse: When agents accumulate memories over many sessions and retrieval quality degrades over time, when memory storage has size constraints, when contradictory or outdated information causes agent confusion, or when you need to extract higher-level patterns from accumulated low-level observations.
strengths: Prevents unbounded memory growth that degrades retrieval quality
Merging redundant memories reduces noise and improves signal-to-noise ratio
Removing contradicted memories prevents agent confusion from stale information
Reflection during consolidation can surface higher-level insights not visible in individual memories
weaknesses: Consolidation itself requires LLM calls, adding cost proportional to memory size
Aggressive pruning risks losing context that turns out to be important later
Detecting semantic similarity between memories is imperfect -- near-duplicates may survive
The consolidation cycle introduces a delay between memory creation and availability of consolidated knowledge

agents.name: Memory Scanner
agents.description: Scans the full memory store to identify candidates for consolidation -- duplicates, contradictions, stale entries, and frequently-accessed patterns

agents.name: Memory Consolidator
agents.description: Merges redundant memories, resolves contradictions by keeping the most recent or most evidenced version, and promotes frequently-accessed patterns to a fast-access tier

agents.name: Reflection Agent
agents.description: Reviews consolidated memories to extract higher-level patterns and insights that span multiple individual memories

shared: Memory store -- the full set of accumulated memories with metadata (creation time, access count, source)
Consolidation log -- record of merges, deletions, and promotions for auditability
private: Reflector's higher-level insights derived from consolidation patterns
memoryArchitecture.description: The memory store is the shared artifact being maintained. The scanner reads it to identify consolidation candidates. The consolidator modifies it by merging, updating, and pruning. The reflector reads the consolidated output to extract higher-level patterns. The consolidation log provides an audit trail. The cycle runs periodically, not on every memory access.

diagram:
graph TD
  TRIGGER[Trigger:\nSchedule / Threshold] --> MS[Memory Scanner]
  STORE[(Memory Store)] <--> MS
  MS -->|duplicates, stale, contradictions| MC[Memory Consolidator]
  MC -->|merge/prune/promote| STORE
  MC -->|consolidated batch| RA[Reflection Agent]
  RA -->|higher-level insights| STORE
  MC -->|audit trail| LOG[(Consolidation Log)]

name: Conversation Summarization Window
summary: Older conversation turns are progressively summarized to fit the context window while recent turns are kept verbatim.
description: As conversation history grows, older turns are progressively summarized to fit within the context window. Recent turns are kept verbatim while older context is compressed into summaries. The summarization preserves key decisions, constraints, and commitments while dropping verbose reasoning. A sliding window divides the conversation into zones: a verbatim recent zone, a summarized middle zone, and a highly compressed or dropped ancient zone. The pattern is triggered when the conversation approaches the context limit.
realWorldExample: Claude Code's auto-compact system summarizes prior context when approaching limits. ChatGPT's conversation memory extracts key facts from long conversations. LangChain's ConversationSummaryBufferMemory keeps recent messages verbatim and summarizes older ones. This is the most common production pattern for long conversations.
whenToUse: When conversations regularly exceed context window limits, when the agent must maintain awareness of earlier decisions across long sessions, when full conversation replay is too expensive, or when you need to balance context retention with token cost.
strengths: Enables arbitrarily long conversations without hitting context window limits
Preserves the most important context (decisions, constraints) while dropping noise
Recent verbatim turns maintain nuance and exact wording for ongoing work
Reduces token cost by compressing older, less relevant context
weaknesses: Summarization is lossy -- important details may be dropped from older context
The summarization step itself costs tokens and adds latency at the compaction boundary
The agent cannot recover verbatim older context once it has been summarized
Summary quality depends on the summarizer's ability to identify what matters, which varies by domain

agents.name: Conversation Manager
agents.description: Monitors context window usage and triggers summarization when approaching limits. Manages the sliding window zones (verbatim, summarized, dropped)

agents.name: Context Summarizer
agents.description: Compresses older conversation turns into concise summaries that preserve key decisions, constraints, commitments, and unresolved questions

agents.name: Primary Agent
agents.description: The main agent handling user requests. Receives a context that blends verbatim recent history with summarized older context

shared: Full conversation log -- the complete verbatim history (stored externally, not in context)
Summary cache -- compressed representations of older conversation segments
private: Conversation manager's context budget tracking and compaction thresholds
memoryArchitecture.description: The full conversation log is the ground truth stored outside the context window. The summary cache contains compressed older segments. The conversation manager assembles the context for each turn: recent verbatim turns + summarized older turns + system prompt. The primary agent sees only the assembled context, not the raw log.

diagram:
graph LR
  USER[User Message] --> CM[Conversation Manager]
  CM -->|check token count| BUDGET{Near Limit?}
  BUDGET -->|no| PA[Primary Agent]
  BUDGET -->|yes| SUM[Context Summarizer]
  SUM -->|compress older turns| SC[(Summary Cache)]
  SC --> CM
  LOG[(Full Conversation Log)] --> CM
  CM -->|assembled context:\nsummary + recent verbatim| PA
  PA -->|response| USER

name: Structured Output Enforcement
summary: The agent is constrained to produce output in a specific format with the LLM provider enforcing structure at the token level.
description: The agent is constrained to produce output in a specific format (JSON schema, typed objects, enum values) with the LLM provider enforcing the structure at the token level. Rather than generating freeform text and hoping it parses, the model's sampling is constrained so that only valid tokens can be generated at each step. This eliminates parsing failures and retry loops for structured data extraction. The pattern shifts validation from post-generation (parse and retry) to in-generation (constrained decoding), making structured outputs reliable by construction rather than by luck.
realWorldExample: OpenAI's structured outputs with JSON schema enforcement. Anthropic's tool use returns structured JSON. Google Gemini's controlled generation. Instructor library wraps LLM calls with Pydantic validation. Outlines and Guidance provide grammar-based constrained generation for open-source models.
whenToUse: When downstream systems require machine-readable structured data, when parsing failures from freeform LLM output cause reliability issues, when the output schema is known at design time, or when you need type-safe integration between LLM agents and deterministic code.
strengths: Eliminates parsing failures -- output is structurally valid by construction
No retry loops needed for format compliance, reducing latency and cost
Type safety at the boundary between LLM and deterministic code
Schema serves as documentation of the expected output contract
weaknesses: Constrained decoding can reduce output quality -- the model may force-fit content into the schema
Complex nested schemas are harder for models to fill correctly even with enforcement
Schema must be defined at design time -- does not work for dynamically-shaped outputs
Provider-specific implementations (OpenAI, Anthropic, Outlines) are not interchangeable

agents.name: Schema Designer
agents.description: Defines and maintains the output schemas (JSON Schema, Pydantic models, or grammar definitions) that constrain agent outputs

agents.name: Constrained Generator
agents.description: The LLM agent that produces output under structural constraints. Its token sampling is restricted to only emit valid tokens per the active schema

agents.name: Validation Agent
agents.description: Performs semantic validation on structurally-valid outputs -- checks that values are reasonable, references exist, and business rules are satisfied beyond what the schema can express

shared: Schema registry -- versioned collection of output schemas with documentation and examples
Validation rules -- semantic rules beyond structural constraints (value ranges, referential integrity)
private: Schema designer's iteration history and schema evolution decisions
memoryArchitecture.description: The schema registry is the shared contract between schema designers, generators, and validators. The constrained generator uses the active schema to restrict its sampling. The validation agent applies semantic rules that go beyond structural correctness. Schemas are versioned to support backward compatibility.

diagram:
graph LR
  IN[Input Request] --> CG[Constrained Generator]
  SR[(Schema Registry)] -.->|active schema| CG
  CG -->|structurally valid output| VA[Validation Agent]
  SR -.->|semantic rules| VA
  VA -->|valid| OUT[Downstream System]
  VA -->|semantic error| CG
  SD[Schema Designer] -->|define/update| SR

name: Tenant-Isolated Agent
summary: The same agent architecture serves multiple tenants with strict data isolation per customer, team, or project.
description: The same agent architecture serves multiple tenants (customers, teams, projects) with strict data isolation. Each tenant has its own knowledge base, memory, tool configurations, and permissions. The agent code is shared but the context is completely separated. Tenant isolation must be enforced at every layer: prompt context, tool access, memory retrieval, API credentials, and output routing. A breach of tenant isolation -- where one customer's data leaks into another's agent context -- is the highest-severity failure mode.
realWorldExample: Salesforce Agentforce serves thousands of enterprise customers with tenant-isolated agent instances. Amazon Bedrock's agent sessions isolate customer data. Intercom's Fin AI agent operates across thousands of customer support environments with strict data boundaries. Every SaaS AI assistant faces this pattern.
whenToUse: When building a SaaS product with AI agents that serves multiple customers, when data privacy regulations require strict separation between tenant data, when different tenants need different configurations of the same agent, or when the cost of per-tenant infrastructure is prohibitive.
strengths: Shared infrastructure reduces cost compared to fully separate deployments per tenant
Consistent agent behavior and updates across all tenants from a single codebase
Per-tenant customization (tools, knowledge, permissions) without code forks
Centralized monitoring and management across the entire tenant fleet
weaknesses: Tenant isolation bugs can leak one customer's data into another's agent context
Noisy neighbor problems -- one tenant's heavy usage can degrade others' performance
Per-tenant customization adds complexity to every layer of the stack
Compliance requirements (data residency, encryption) may vary by tenant

agents.name: Tenant Router
agents.description: Identifies the requesting tenant from authentication context and routes to the correct isolated agent instance with tenant-specific configuration

agents.name: Isolated Agent Instance
agents.description: An agent instance scoped to a single tenant. Sees only that tenant's knowledge base, memory, tools, and permissions. Cannot access other tenants' data

agents.name: Configuration Manager
agents.description: Manages per-tenant configurations -- which tools are enabled, knowledge base sources, custom instructions, permission policies, and usage quotas

agents.name: Isolation Auditor
agents.description: Continuously monitors for isolation violations -- cross-tenant data leaks, unauthorized tool access, or context contamination between tenants

shared: Tenant registry -- authentication mappings, configuration pointers, and quota status per tenant
Shared agent codebase -- the common agent logic deployed across all tenants
private: Per-tenant knowledge base -- documents, FAQs, and domain data owned by each tenant
Per-tenant memory -- conversation history, learned preferences, and session state
Per-tenant credentials -- API keys, tool configurations, and integration secrets
memoryArchitecture.description: The tenant router and configuration manager share a tenant registry. Each isolated agent instance has strictly private access to its tenant's data, memory, and credentials. The isolation auditor monitors boundaries. The only shared layer is the agent codebase itself -- all data is tenant-scoped.

diagram:
graph TD
  REQ[Incoming Request] --> AUTH[Authentication]
  AUTH -->|tenant ID| TR[Tenant Router]
  CFG[(Tenant Config Registry)] -.-> TR
  TR -->|tenant A| IA1[Isolated Agent A]
  TR -->|tenant B| IA2[Isolated Agent B]
  TR -->|tenant C| IA3[Isolated Agent C]
  KBA[(KB: Tenant A)] <--> IA1
  KBB[(KB: Tenant B)] <--> IA2
  KBC[(KB: Tenant C)] <--> IA3
  AUDIT[Isolation Auditor] -.->|monitor| IA1
  AUDIT -.->|monitor| IA2
  AUDIT -.->|monitor| IA3

name: Role-Based Agent Permissions
summary: Agents operate under role-based access control with declared permissions for tools, files, and APIs per role.
description: Agents operate under role-based access control -- different agents (or the same agent in different contexts) have different permissions for which tools they can call, which files they can access, and which APIs they can invoke. Permissions are declared in configuration rather than embedded in prompts. A permission enforcement layer intercepts every tool call and checks it against the agent's role before execution. This prevents privilege escalation in multi-agent systems where a compromised or confused agent should not access everything.
realWorldExample: Claude Code's permission system with allow/deny rules per tool category. AWS IAM roles applied to Bedrock agent sessions. Semantic Kernel's agent authorization filters. The pattern prevents agents from exceeding their intended scope, especially in multi-agent systems where a compromised agent shouldn't access everything.
whenToUse: When agents in the same system need different levels of access, when security requires defense-in-depth beyond prompt instructions, when regulatory compliance demands auditable access control, or when multi-agent systems need to enforce the principle of least privilege.
strengths: Defense-in-depth -- permissions are enforced at the tool layer, not just in prompts
Auditable -- every permission check is logged with agent role, tool, and allow/deny decision
Configurable without code changes -- role definitions are declarative configuration
Prevents privilege escalation in multi-agent systems where agents invoke other agents
weaknesses: Overly restrictive permissions can prevent agents from completing legitimate tasks
Permission configuration complexity grows with the number of roles and tools
The enforcement layer adds latency to every tool call
Role boundaries may not map cleanly to task requirements -- agents may need temporary elevation

agents.name: Permission Manager
agents.description: Defines and maintains role-permission mappings. Creates roles with specific tool, file, and API access levels. Handles permission change requests and audits

agents.name: Permission Enforcement Layer
agents.description: Intercepts every tool call from every agent, checks the calling agent's role against the permission policy, and allows or denies execution. Logs all decisions

agents.name: Restricted Agent
agents.description: Any operational agent that operates under a specific role with defined permissions. Cannot invoke tools outside its role's allowed set

agents.name: Elevation Handler
agents.description: Handles requests for temporary permission elevation when an agent needs access beyond its role. May require human approval or multi-agent consensus

shared: Permission policy -- role definitions with tool/file/API allow and deny lists
Access log -- timestamped record of every permission check with role, tool, and decision
private: Restricted agent's task context (does not include permission policy details)
Elevation handler's approval history and precedent database
memoryArchitecture.description: The permission policy is the shared authority. The enforcement layer reads it on every tool call. The access log accumulates audit entries. Restricted agents are unaware of the enforcement mechanism -- they make tool calls normally and receive allow or deny responses. Elevation requests are queued for the handler.

diagram:
graph TD
  RA1[Agent: Reader Role] -->|read file| EL[Permission Enforcement Layer]
  RA2[Agent: Writer Role] -->|write file| EL
  RA3[Agent: Admin Role] -->|delete file| EL
  PP[(Permission Policy)] -.->|role rules| EL
  EL -->|allowed| TOOLS[Tool Execution]
  EL -->|denied| DENY[Access Denied]
  EL -->|log| AL[(Access Log)]
  RA2 -->|elevation request| EH[Elevation Handler]
  EH -->|temporary grant| PP

name: World Model Agent
summary: The agent maintains an internal model of the environment and plans by simulating action sequences before executing them.
description: The agent maintains an internal model of the environment's state and uses it to predict outcomes of actions before executing them. The world model is updated based on observations from the real environment. The agent plans by simulating action sequences in the model rather than trial-and-error in the real environment. This enables look-ahead reasoning: the agent can evaluate multiple possible action sequences in simulation and select the most promising one before committing to real execution. The world model is the key differentiator from reactive agents that only respond to current observations.
realWorldExample: DeepMind's Dreamer and DreamerV3 learn world models for game playing. NVIDIA's Cosmos platform provides world foundation models for physical AI. Yann LeCun's JEPA (Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture) is a proposal for world models in AI systems. World models are the theoretical foundation for planning-capable agents.
whenToUse: When actions in the real environment are expensive, slow, or irreversible, when the agent needs to plan multiple steps ahead, when the environment dynamics are learnable from observation, or when comparing alternative action sequences before committing is valuable.
strengths: Plans without real-world consequences -- simulated actions cost nothing and risk nothing
Multi-step lookahead enables strategic behavior beyond greedy one-step decisions
The world model captures dynamics that the agent can exploit for planning
Model accuracy improves with more observations, creating a flywheel effect
weaknesses: World model inaccuracy leads to plans that fail when executed in reality (model error compounding)
Learning an accurate world model requires extensive interaction data
Simulation-based planning is computationally expensive -- evaluating many action sequences takes time
The agent may exploit world model errors rather than learning genuine strategies

agents.name: World Modeler
agents.description: Maintains and updates the internal representation of the environment. Takes observations as input and produces predicted next states for proposed actions

agents.name: Planning Agent
agents.description: Uses the world model to simulate and evaluate multiple action sequences. Selects the best plan based on predicted outcomes and expected reward

agents.name: Action Executor
agents.description: Executes the selected plan in the real environment one step at a time, feeding observations back to the world modeler for model updates

agents.name: State Observer
agents.description: Captures observations from the real environment after each action and feeds them to the world modeler for model correction and the planner for replanning

shared: World model -- the learned representation of environment dynamics (state transitions, reward predictions)
Current state estimate -- the agent's best understanding of the environment's current state
private: Planner's search tree of evaluated action sequences and their predicted outcomes
World modeler's training data buffer of observation-action-outcome triples
memoryArchitecture.description: The world model is the central shared artifact. The planner reads it to simulate action sequences. The observer provides real observations that update the model. The executor carries out plans in the real environment. The key loop is: observe → update model → plan in simulation → execute best plan → observe result → update model.

diagram:
graph TD
  OBS[State Observer] -->|observations| WM[World Modeler]
  WM -->|updated model| PA[Planning Agent]
  PA -->|simulate action sequences| WM
  WM -->|predicted outcomes| PA
  PA -->|best plan| EX[Action Executor]
  EX -->|action| ENV[Real Environment]
  ENV -->|feedback| OBS
  PA -->|replan if prediction error| WM

name: Agent Compiler Target
summary: Agent behavior is defined in a high-level DSL and compiled down to optimized LLM calls, tool invocations, and control flow.
description: Instead of prompting an LLM to be an agent, the agent behavior is defined in a high-level DSL (domain-specific language) and compiled down to optimized LLM calls, tool invocations, and control flow. The compilation step can optimize for cost, latency, or accuracy. The agent definition is declarative -- it specifies what the agent should do, not how. The compiler handles prompt optimization, example selection, caching strategies, and call scheduling. This separates agent design (what) from agent execution (how), letting different compilation backends optimize for different deployment targets.
realWorldExample: DSPy compiles Python signatures into optimized prompts and demonstrations. SGLang compiles agent programs into optimized inference schedules with RadixAttention for prefix sharing. Salesforce Agentforce uses declarative YAML to define agents that compile to runtime configurations. The pattern separates agent design from agent execution.
whenToUse: When you want to iterate on agent behavior without hand-tuning prompts, when optimization across cost/latency/quality trade-offs needs to be systematic rather than manual, when the same agent definition should deploy to different backends, or when prompt engineering is a bottleneck in agent development.
strengths: Separates agent design from execution -- change the compiler, not the agent definition
Systematic optimization across cost, latency, and quality trade-offs
Portable agent definitions that compile to different LLM backends
Eliminates manual prompt engineering through automated optimization
weaknesses: The compilation abstraction can obscure what the LLM actually receives, making debugging harder
Compiler optimization requires evaluation data -- cold-start performance may be poor
DSL expressiveness is limited -- novel agent behaviors may require escaping the DSL
Compilation adds a build step and toolchain complexity

agents.name: DSL Author
agents.description: Writes agent behavior specifications in the high-level declarative language. Defines inputs, outputs, constraints, and quality criteria

agents.name: Agent Compiler
agents.description: Transforms declarative agent specifications into optimized execution plans -- selects prompts, examples, caching strategies, and call schedules based on the optimization target (cost, latency, quality)

agents.name: Compiled Runtime
agents.description: Executes the compiled agent plan. Manages LLM calls, tool invocations, control flow, and result aggregation according to the compiled specification

agents.name: Compilation Evaluator
agents.description: Runs compiled agents against evaluation sets to measure quality. Provides feedback to the compiler for iterative optimization of prompts and examples

shared: Agent specifications -- declarative definitions of agent behavior in the DSL
Compiled artifacts -- optimized execution plans, cached prompts, and selected examples
Evaluation datasets -- input-output pairs for measuring compilation quality
private: Compiler's optimization history and prompt/example search state
memoryArchitecture.description: The DSL author writes specifications that the compiler transforms into execution plans. The compiled runtime executes plans without knowledge of the source DSL. The evaluator measures runtime output quality and feeds results back to the compiler. The compilation is iterative -- each evaluation round improves the compiled output.

diagram:
graph LR
  DSL[DSL Author] -->|agent spec| COMP[Agent Compiler]
  EVAL[(Evaluation Set)] -.-> COMP
  COMP -->|optimized plan| RT[Compiled Runtime]
  RT -->|LLM calls| LLM[LLM Provider]
  RT -->|tool calls| TOOLS[Tool Layer]
  LLM -->|results| RT
  TOOLS -->|results| RT
  RT -->|output| EV[Compilation Evaluator]
  EV -->|quality metrics| COMP

name: Adversarial Curriculum Learning
summary: A generator agent creates increasingly difficult test cases for a student agent, adapting based on the student's weaknesses.
description: A generator agent creates increasingly difficult test cases for a student agent. The generator adapts based on the student's weaknesses -- if the student masters one type of problem, the generator shifts to harder variants or new failure modes. Both agents improve through the adversarial dynamic: the student learns to handle harder problems while the generator learns to find the student's blind spots. The curriculum is emergent rather than manually designed, automatically targeting the frontier of the student's competence. This creates a co-evolutionary dynamic where both agents improve.
realWorldExample: OpenAI's RLHF process implicitly implements this -- human evaluators focus on cases where the model struggles. Anthropic's red-teaming methodology targets known weak areas. In research, PAIRED (Protagonist Antagonist Induced Regret Environment Design) and PLR (Prioritized Level Replay) generate adversarial training environments. Meta's Hyperagents curriculum is a simplified version of this.
whenToUse: When manually designing test cases cannot keep up with agent improvement, when the failure mode space is too large to enumerate, when you want training to automatically focus on the hardest problems, or when co-evolution of attacker and defender is valuable (as in security hardening).
strengths: Automatically focuses training on the student's weakest areas
The curriculum adapts as the student improves -- no manual difficulty tuning needed
Co-evolutionary dynamics produce more robust agents than static training sets
The generator discovers failure modes that human testers might miss
weaknesses: The generator may create adversarial examples that exploit model artifacts rather than genuine weaknesses
Co-evolution can collapse into degenerate dynamics where neither agent improves meaningfully
Training instability -- the adversarial dynamic can oscillate rather than converge
Evaluating whether the curriculum is actually improving the student requires external benchmarks

agents.name: Curriculum Generator
agents.description: Creates test cases at the frontier of the student's ability. Observes student performance to identify weaknesses and generates harder or novel problem types targeting those weaknesses

agents.name: Student Agent
agents.description: The agent being trained. Attempts to solve generated test cases and improves through the curriculum. Its performance metrics guide the generator's adaptation

agents.name: Evaluation Judge
agents.description: Objectively scores the student's performance on each test case. Provides ground-truth evaluation that both the generator and student depend on for accurate feedback

agents.name: Difficulty Controller
agents.description: Manages the pace of curriculum difficulty escalation. Prevents the generator from jumping too far ahead (making the curriculum frustrating) or staying too easy (wasting training cycles)

shared: Performance log -- the student's scores on each test case, enabling the generator to identify weaknesses
Difficulty schedule -- the current difficulty target and escalation rate
private: Generator's model of student weaknesses and its strategy for creating challenging test cases
Student's learned skills and internal representation of solved problem types
memoryArchitecture.description: The performance log is the shared signal that drives the adversarial dynamic. The generator reads it to find weaknesses. The difficulty controller reads it to calibrate escalation pace. The student and generator each have private models that improve through the co-evolutionary process. The judge provides the objective ground truth.

diagram:
graph TD
  CG[Curriculum Generator] -->|test cases| SA[Student Agent]
  SA -->|attempts| JU[Evaluation Judge]
  JU -->|scores| PL[(Performance Log)]
  PL -->|weaknesses| CG
  PL -->|progress| DC[Difficulty Controller]
  DC -->|difficulty target| CG
  CG -->|harder variants| SA
